title: Home truths for SMSF property investors in race to beat the ban
full_text:
For residential property investors, the post-budget strategy was clear. Leverage up and purchase using a self-managed super fund and when you retire after age 60, enjoy tax-free rental income and tax-free capital gains.
But in a last-minute deal between Labor and the Greens, this strategy is now out the window.
The big question now is what is the best way to structure your finances to grow wealth.
If you’ve been thinking about buying a residential property in super using a mix of cash and debt, you need to act now, even though the exact start date and terms are still unclear.
Act now on SMSF ban The ban on super property fund lending is scheduled to take effect 45 days after the legislation comes into law. With the legislation expected to pass the Senate shortly, it will probably receive royal assent sometime in early July.
This puts the deadline for anyone wanting to gear up and buy in an SMSF at about mid-August. But it is unclear at this stage whether the deadline refers to the property contract date or the settlement date.
If you do not have an SMSF, you are probably out of luck. Although technically possible, there is a lot to get done in six weeks and the ship may have sailed. It usually takes up to a month to first activate an SMSF using an accountant, and another couple of weeks for the rollovers to be received. And then you need to seek pre-approval for an SMSF loan, find a property to buy, negotiate the price, confirm the lender is satisfied with the property as security, conduct due diligence, such as building and pest inspection and contract review, pay the deposit and exchange contracts.
If you already have an SMSF, although you have a head start, securing an SMSF property with a loan will still be a stretch in what is now the 11th hour. The first step is to seek financial advice on the appropriateness of taking out a loan in your super fund and how the property fits into your wealth strategy. If OK, then you should liaise with a mortgage broker, conveyancer and buyer’s agent to get the ball rolling, with the goal to settle by mid-August.
But before jumping into action, it is worth noting a few exemptions that may release the pressure valve and mean you do not need to run around like a headless chicken between now and the expected mid-August cut-off.
What you can still do Commercial property can still be purchased in an SMSF with a loan. The Greens were primarily concerned about investors snapping up residential property and left this alone.
Up to this point, commercial property in super has been a popular strategy for small business owners who wish to become their own landlord. The commercial property would be purchased in super and rented to their trading entity. Usually a related party is prohibited from renting from the SMSF; however, a business real property exemption permits this for commercial properties.
After several years of negative and sluggish price growth, expect conditions to improve for commercial property as it becomes the final frontier for SMSF lending and attracts more attention from investors.
Residential investment property can still be purchased in a SMSF if purchased outright. Rather than the average SMSF using $300,000-$500,000 cash deposit to purchase a leveraged-up $750,000-$1m property investment, we are likely to see a tilt towards lower-priced property markets as SMSF trustees continue to purchase residential properties with no debt. This may spark interest in regional properties and push their markets up.
Outside super The budget hit property investors hard and changed the property investing landscape. Family investment companies with multiple classes of shareholders now seem to be the vehicle of choice from accountants, given the 30 per cent minimum tax imposed on discretionary family trusts, due to start in July 2028.
However, apply a high level of caution if considering this approach, and expert taxation advice is recommended given the ATO’s strong stance on Part IVA tax avoidance.
In other words, if you manipulate dividends from a family investment company by picking and choosing which family members receive them to optimise everyone’s tax position, you may fall foul of the ATO. The ATO may cancel a tax benefit if the arrangement’s dominant purpose is to obtain that benefit rather than to achieve a genuine commercial or family objective.
For the average property investor who is not interested in the complexities of family trusts and companies, and wishes to invest in their personal name, negative gearing will be gone for future purchases once legislated. And with a 30 per cent minimum CGT applied in addition to a less generous inflation indexation formula, it is becoming slim pickings.
Either purchase a brand-new residential property or commercial property if you want to negatively gear in your personal name, otherwise consider high-yield property such as dual-income, short-term rentals and boarding houses. There is a trade-off between income and growth, and going for higher property income means less growth but also less CGT down the track. And by obtaining higher income, depending on your level of borrowing on the property, you are more likely to be cashflow neutral and not miss out on the last negative gearing tax benefits that you would have received in the past.
As the list of winners from the budget gets smaller and smaller, with a line now through SMSF property investors following the Greens’ Senate deal, commercial property owners are among the few winners. Expect investor interest to increase in this area given its exemption from negative gearing and SMSF lending changes. James Gerrard is principal and director of financial planning firm www.financialadvisor.com.au
